content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 16, 2016

David M. Snow
Richards Watson Gershon
355 South Grand Avenue, 40th Floor
Los Angeles, California 90071-3101

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-137

Dear Mr. Snow:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Mayor Denise Hoyt regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTIONS

1. Does Mayor Hoyt have a disqualifying conflict of interest that precludes her from participating in the City Council's deliberations and a decision regarding the City of Yucaipa's Housing Element Ordinance?

2. Does Mayor Hoyt have a disqualifying conflict of interest that precludes her from participating in the City Council's deliberations and a decision regarding the pending conditional use permit to establish a residential care facility on a property located approximately 890 feet from her residency?

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. Mayor Hoyt does not have a conflict of interest that precludes her from participating in the City Council's deliberations and a decision regarding the City of Yucaipa's Housing Element Ordinance.

2. No. Mayor Hoyt does not have a conflict of interest that precludes her from participating in the City Council's deliberations and a decision regarding the pending conditional use permit.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-16-137
Page No. 2

FACTS

The City Council will be considering an ordinance implementing the City's Housing
Element ("Housing Ordinance"). The Housing Ordinance would:

• Update definitions related to small and large residential care facilities, boarding houses,
single housekeeping units, motels, hotels, and lodging houses

• Update regulations regarding residential care facilities and boarding houses

• Determine where such uses may be permitted within the city.

• Would prohibit care facilities serving more than 6 residents from locating within single
family residential zones. According to information you provided in a telephone
conversation, approximately 63.5 percent of the city's residential real property is in single
family residential zones.

The City Council is also considering an application for a conditional use permit to operate a
large residential care facility as a drug and alcohol recovery treatment facility serving seven or more
residents on property currently zoned RS-20, a single-family dwelling zone that allows density of
up to 2.2 dwelling units per acre. This parcel is currently occupied by a small, State-licensed,
residential care facility (serving six or fewer residents). According to the conditional use permit
application, the proposal would not require any changes or additions to the buildings on the
property at this time, but the proposed conditional use permit would allow the facility to serve two
additional residents (for a total of eight residents). The application states that future plans will be to
construct another residence on the particular property, as allowed by the city code, to house the
applicant's owners. The conditional use permit also requests approval to host quarterly BBQs and
family gatherings for residents, and overnight motorhome parking and open house gatherings up to
four times per year.

The property consists of an approximately 3.8-acre parcel, with a 4 bedroom, 2.75-bathroom
home. The parcel includes a pool, lighted tennis court, and a paved area for motorhome or boat
parking. Mayor Hoyt owns a residence located approximately 890 feet from the conditional use
permit applicant property, and both properties are located within the single family residential zone,
RS-20.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest rules prohibit a public official from making, participating in
making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a governmental decision in
which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a "financial interest." (Section
87100.) A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87103
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or
on any one of five enumerated economic interests. (Section 87103.)


File No. A-16-137
Page No. 3

Financial Interest

The interest here is residential real property in which Mayor Hoyt has an interest of $2,000
or more. (Section 87103(b).)

Decision involving the Housing Ordinance

Mayor Hoyt's residence is located in a single family residential zone which would be
subject to the Housing Ordinance and would therefore presumably would be foreseeably and materially
affected by the ordinance. However, pursuant to the "public generally" exception, the Mayor may
still participate in the decision. The public generally exception applies only if the effect of a
decision on the official's interest is indistinguishable for the effect on the public. Generally, an
effect on the official is indistinguishable from the effect on the public if the official establishes that
a significant segment of the public is affected and that there is no unique effect on the official.
(Regulation 18703.)

A "significant segment," as applied to the facts here, is at least 25% of all residential real
property within the City of Yucaipa. (Regulation 18703(b)(2).) The Housing Ordinance would
prohibit residential care facilities serving more than six people from locating in single family
residential zones, applies to all properties located in these zones; and thus applies to approximately
63.5% of all residential properties within the City. While Mayor Hoyt has an interest in residential
property, there is no evidence that her property will experience a disproportionate or unique effect.
Because the public generally exception applies to Mayor Hoyt, she may participate in the decisions
related to the Ordinance.

Decision involving the conditional use permit

Foreseeability and Materiality

A financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable when it is explicitly involved in
a decision. Financial interests that are explicitly involved in a decision include an interest that is a
named party in, or subject of, a government decision, and includes any governmental decision
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6). (Regulation
18701(a).) Mayor Hoyt is not a named party in, or subject of, the conditional use permit application,
and is not specified in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6) and is therefore not explicitly involved in the
decision.

For interests that are not explicitly involved, as here, the effect need not be likely to be
considered reasonably foreseeable. "In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable."
(Regulation 18701(b).)

For real property not explicitly involved in a decision and pertinent to your facts, a
foreseeable effect is material if the decision:

I'm unable to transcribe any text from this image as it appears to be completely blank or contains only white space. If there is text present that should be visible, the image may need to be re-submitted with better contrast or quality.

File No. A-16-137
Page No. 4

(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially
altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property
surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or
air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market
value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest;

. . .

(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration
under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a
nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of
the official's property.

(Regulation 18702.2)

The parcel that is the subject of the conditional use permit is currently occupied by a small
residential care facility, serving six or fewer residents. As the property is already operating as a
residential treatment facility, the decision to allow up to two additional residents, and the hosting of
BBQs and gatherings up to four times a year, would not substantially alter traffic levels in the area
surrounding the property. Although application also states that future plans will be to construct
another residence on the property to house the applicant's owners, this is already allowed per city
code.

Based the nature and magnitude of the proposed change, including the fact the property is
currently operating as a residential care facility serving up to six residents, on an approximately 3.8-
acre parcel, it does not appear that the decision to permit the addition of two additional residents
will have any impact on parking, views, privacy, noise levels, air quality; or influence the market
value of Mayor Hoyt's property (Regulation 18702.2(a)(10) and (12).) Based on the facts provided,
and considering the nature of the existing use, it would appear that the effect of the decision will not
have a foreseeable and material financial effect on Mayor Hoyt's real property.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
